content.full_text:
Transportation and Air Status Solutions

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel                    July 23, 1991
Attention: Mark T. Morodomi, Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800, P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am writing to ask you promptly to send me full documents and
scoping information on the Commission's enforcement action against the
Folsom City Council, FPPC #87-242, and to review the summary dismissal
of my letter July 7, 1991 with a form letter and nothing thereafter
until I telephoned, and then only what I could fully identify and not
what FPPC had and was reached for in my two-page letter.  A copy is
enclosed.  From the decision which was mailed after I telephoned,
I see that you have other decisions related to my inquiry which my
letter manifestly reached for, and I expressed time of the essence,
which the delayed mailing of the form letter and only one document
after I telephoned were not a sensitive response to.

Just because Blanca M. Breeze wanted or wants to send Complaint
form as a substitute--after I told her that I already had one, is, in
my view, not a justification for the legal division short-shifting
response to my letter.  She did omit mailing the statutes book when I
told her on the telephone that she needn't because I had it x the c.form.
When I ask for "not only with the advice letter #I-90-662a and
related, but with wherever you can say on the Sand Hill Corridor matter"
even if your counsel wants to avoid specifically instructing me on the
Sand Hill Corridor matter, I think she reasonably in response to my
letter should promptly have so written me and sent me the advice
letter #I-90-662a, the Folsom FPPC #87-242 materials by which I can
understand that, copies of the regulations mentioned enclosed with
the City of Isleta informal assistance, and other regulations and
decisions you may have but which I do not know, for a specifically
responsive answer to my letter, instead of essentially ignoring it
and pushing general publications such book of statutes and
a complaint form.  If members of the public are to be induced to
put more in for enforcement at their expense, to you, the first step
for FPPC to take is to give serious attention to their two-page letter
extinguishing awareness of two decisions and asking for more, rather than
expecting them, instead, to write more after injury.  Or would you
rather by default in accommodating violation of law,
stimulate grist for enforcement actions?  Champerty and maintenance.

I'm not charging FPPC with intent to do wrong, which would of
course be vigorously denied, just with having opportunities to do
better in regards pointed to herein.

If FPPC wants people to pay 10¢/page why doesn't it say this in
the bulletin or is the number of pages in the Advice Letter
Summaries and Enforcement Action articles for each document involved?
--instead of burdening people with long distance phone call to find out
--instead of FPPC delay in answering their letters?

Sincerely yours,

[signature] Nancy Jewell Cross

Enclosure, my letter dated 7/7/91,
and Meet the Issues 7/22/91.

CLEAN AIR TRANSPORT SYSTEMS · (415) 854-6882 · 301 Vine Street · Menlo Park, CA 94025
Dr. Nancy Jewell Cross, Chief Executive Officer

PRE-APPLICATION AGREEMENT ("MOU") FOR CITY
TO REFUND HALF ITS COST OF PROCESSING AN
APPLICATION FROM A PARTICULAR LAND DEVELOPER
DIFFERENTLY FROM OTHERS IF THE CITY DOESN'T
APPROVE CONSTRUCTION, VIOLATES STATE FAIR
POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION RULINGS

At Palo Alto City Council Meeting                                July 22, 1991
Palo Alto City Hall
250 Hamilton Avenue              Re: Agenda Item #20, CMR 377:91
Palo Alto, CA 94301                  City of Palo Alto-Stanford University
                                     Sand Hill Corridor Study Agreement

To Mayor Jack Sutorius and Council
Statement of Dr. Nancy Jewell Cross
CLEAN AIR TRANSPORT SYSTEMS
Chief Executive Officer

        The Sand Hill Corridor funding agreement proposed between the

City and Stanford University is, in my view, inconsistent with law, in

several deficits therefrom.

        Respectful of the council's alarm cutoff of speakers from this

microphone at five minutes, I have chosen for the communication bit or

byte on the Agreement to address its Section (4) wherein the City of

Palo Alto agrees to reimburse Stanford University for half of all funds

advanced by the university if the city terminates the project prior to

a Notice of Determination being filed.

        As you are aware, the informed community has reacted adversely to

the rebate provision.      Is it, nonetheless, not perfectly legal for

the city to agree prior to a favored developer's filing of an applica-

tion for permit to construct, and as an inducement to its filing, to

self-penalize for a city decision against the project?


                                                Pre-Application Agreement Between
CLEAN AIR TRANSPORT SYSTEMS
Association of Civic Shareholders      City and Land Developer for Rebate:
Transportation and Air Status Solutions
Dr. Nancy Jewell Cross, Chief Executive Officer
(415) 854-6882 : 301 Vine Street                           Is It Legal?
Menlo Park, California 94025
                                                           See over.

The question is cogently answered in an advice letter from
California Fair Political Practices Commission to Lee Savage, of the
City of Isleton, summarized in the July 1991 issue of FPPC Bulletin,
quoting from the advice letter, dated May 8, 1991,
on page 11.
FPPC #1-90-662a, six pages in all, at pages 4 and 5:

"The question here is whether a developer who pays monies to
a city so that the city may hire consultants to process the developer's
application is a 'source of income' to the consultants. If it is a
source of income to the consultants, the developer must be disclosed
as such and may be cause for a conflict of interest for the consultants.

"You make reference to a past Commission enforcement action
against the Folsom City Council involving similar circumstances.
(FPPC #87-242, 11/7/90.) In the Folsom action, the developer paid
money into a special city account which was earmarked for the hiring
and compensation of planning consultants. The consultants were hired
solely for the developer's project, devoted their time only to reviewing
the developer's applications, and were paid only with money provided by
the developer. One consultant billed the city each month for his
time, and each month the city turned around and billed the developer in
order to pay the consultant.

"Normally, where a city pays compensation to a public official,
the compensation is not an economic interest that must be disclosed or
will cause a conflict of interest. (California Government Code,
Section 82030.) However under these facts, the other than the
source of compensation for the public official, was merely a conduit
for the developer. The developers controlled the flow of income to
the consultants through their decisions to pay bills.
The developers also could have influenced the consultants' decisions by
threatening to cancel the project thereby eliminating the consultants'
jobs. The arrangement created a situation which essentially
threatened the impartiality of the consultants and, at minimum, created
the unseemly appearance of a conflict of interest. The consultants
were, in effect, being paid by the developer to make governmental
decisions affecting the developer's project. The developer thus was
a source of income to the consultants.

"To avoid such a situation, the developer who pays for the hiring
and compensation of public officials cannot be, in law or in fact a
source of income to the public officials. The developer cannot in
any way control the flow of income to the consultants. The city
cannot merely be a conduit for the developer. In light of these
concerns, we give the following advice.

"The city must have the sole discretion to hire, fire and direct
consultants and staff who work on the developer's project. Any
compensation the consultants receive must in no way be controlled by
the developer. Any payments made by the developer to the city should
not be tied to the ultimate outcome of the project; payments should not
be waived or returned should the project be disapproved." (End of quote.)

Yes, you heard correctly: "payments should not be
waived or returned should the project be disapproved."

The agreement proposed for your Mayor's signature for the Council
providing the land developer a rebate in case the city disapproved its
project is forbidden by law, as determined by recent decisions of
California Fair Political Practices Commission, on comparable facts.

2

Transportation and Air Status Solutions

Blanca M. Breeze, Counsel
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800, P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

July 7, 1991

Re: Your Files:
#I-90-622a - Letter to Lee Savage, Sacramento, dated May 8, 1991.
FPPC Bulletin July 1991 Advice Letter Summary on page 11.
The City of Isleton wishes to charge developers the actual
cost of processing their land use applications, including the
cost of consultants, city staff and application fees charged by
LAFCO. This letter sets forth procedures and conditions whereby
developers may fund these costs without being considered sources
of income to consultants and city staff and thus not cause for
conflict of interest.

#A-90-292 - Letter to Ariel Pierre Calonne, City of Palo Alto, 10/23/90.
On duties and responsibilities of joint policy committee installed
for land developer's project in the Sand Hill Corridor, under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.

Dear M. Breeze:

I would appreciate your sending to me as soon as this letter
reaches you, the advice letter and full file to date in #I-90-622a,
and as soon as you can after that an application of the aforesaid more
recent decision to the situation brought to your attention in A-90-292
request and updated here.

The question on Sand Hill Road Corridor in light of the City of
Isleton advice letter is: Can a city contract with a land developer
to remit half the fees paid by the developer to cover costs of processing its application (whereas other developers must pay the whole) on
procedural framework/alternatives and environmental impact report-
preparing consultant chosen by ad hoc committees of city controllers/
staff of two cities and the land developer's executives before the
land developer files an application with the City of Palo Alto's
Planning Department, if at any point before approving the land developer's project, the City decides against it, without the developer being
a source of income to the consultant and city staff?

City of Palo Alto has at the land developer's initiative set up
committees including 2 Stanford administrators and its own city council
members and Menlo Park city council members in equal numbers, from
approximately 1975, to advise parameters for land development before
public hearings in environmental impact report processing, with the
impact to denigrate the significance of public input in whether the
development is approved. Consultant selection committees made of
public agen[illegible] staff and Stanford University staff meet privately out
of public view to endorse the choice of the land developer by the
latter as agency decision. By agency adopted Memorandum of Understanding where county approvals are involved, the public agency decisions on parameters, consultant, and form of decision on the project
are made by staff, privately, of County of Santa Clara, City of

CLEAN AIR TRANSPORT SYSTEMS · (415) 854-6882 · 301 Vine Street · Menlo Park, CA 94025
Dr. Nancy Jewell Cross, Chief Executive Officer

Letter to Bianca M. Breeze, July 7, 1991

Palo Alto, and Stanford University, which the County Planning Commission then pro forma ratifies at its meetings, with public input ignored for all practical purposes. The public hasn't been able to get rid of the rigging, and has had to fight through the prejudgments of planning commissioners, city councilors, and county supervisors based on distorted facts in huge volumes developed by financially-conflicted consultants and city staff to confuse and blind deciders from knowing relevant, material facts timely before they become committed to approval of the project. It is difficult enough to address planning commissions and city councils and boards of supervisors after the developer-led promotions for several years involving city councilmen in illegitimate joint committees; and people know their purpose to manipulate civic decisions by securing city councilmembers' commitments in advance of legitimate processing of a land development application from Stanford University, avoid being victimized in their own decision-making by boycotting them, and anticipating that any legitimate hearings and decisions will take place after Stanford University has filed a formal application for whatever project.

Your advice letter, I have an idea, is premised on the Sand Hill Road Corridor Joint Policy Committee's proceedings relating to a real project, real by having been preceded by an application to the City of Palo Alto Planning Department, and that the joint policy committee smarted when the formality of having a "charter" form occurred in 1987. The pre-application machinations in this third round, of which the first round extended from outset in 1975 to demise in 1978, began about with the charter and are still going on in 1991, four years later! Stanford University hasn't reached satisfaction with the rigging enough to file an application to the City of Palo Alto Planning Department for the land development if it seems-for the third time, and there's no justification in my view for public participation before then. The joint committees have had different names over the years and different functioned. The most recent city attorneys for City of Palo Alto haven't been around long enough to know the historical facts from experience or had incentive to research them to let you know adversely to the advice they want from you! I have had a substantial participant experience in the subject matter from 1975 from inception of the first round. In this letter I am concerned not to burden you with facts not material to your role for the Fair Political Practices Commission, so I have spoken mostly only on what appears materially misconceived from misinformation from City of Palo Alto, albeit not necessarily with intent to convey falsely, and ask you to let me know if you want supporting particulars for anything here asserted, or information on other matters you believe may have bearing for your full and accurate assessment.

The City of Palo Alto is in process of subscribing two contracts, possibly on July 22, 1991, to carry out what is questioned in the second paragraph of this letter, on page 1, and I would very much appreciate hearing from you before then not only with the advice letter #1-90-622a and related, but with whatever you can say on the Sand Hill Corridor matter. If you contact the City Attorney, he is likely to try to have you dismiss concern by saying the City has "abolished" the Sand Hill Joint Policy Committee, and withholding the fact that the anticipated two contracts are fruit of the poisoned treet.

Sincerely yours,

[Signature: Nancy Jewell Oaks]

Breeze wanted or wants to send Complaint form as a substitute--after I told her that I already had one, is, in my view, not a justification for the legal division short-shrifting response to my letter. She did omit mailing the statutes book when I told her on the telephone that she needn't because I had it & the c.form.
